quotes.verbatim_quote:
Triads - 1st - 2nd And Root - Melodic minor up, middle voice - harmonic minor down, lower voice -
run each one separately in both directions-reverse them also=Harmonic middie voice-melodic lower

voice etc.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the 'mixed minors' device — combining melodic-minor ascent in one voice with harmonic-minor descent in another — that names and powers the entire chapter.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This is anexample of changing registers because of range
quotes.why_load_bearing: Flags Van Eps's deliberate register-shift technique when a voice-pair runs out of physical fingerboard range, a recurring mechanism in long mixed-minor lines.

quotes.verbatim_quote: These are substitute fingerings for the very long 2nd interval spread - however, try to play them as is
quotes.why_load_bearing: Encodes the Van Eps practice norm: substitute fingerings exist for awkward wide spreads, but the student should attempt the original spread first as a stretch builder.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Extend all of these studies full range
quotes.why_load_bearing: Single-line directive that the printed examples are seeds — the student is expected to push each mixed-minor pattern across the entire fingerboard.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Triads - 1st inversion - 2nd inversion - Root position - melodic up - harmonic down
quotes.why_load_bearing: Compresses the chapter's exercise architecture: all three inversions × melodic-ascending against harmonic-descending — the mixed-minor matrix.

quotes.verbatim_quote: All the practical duplicates are shown below - look for them in middle and lower voice motion also
quotes.why_load_bearing: Directs the student to hunt for duplicate voicings in every voice — not just the upper line — extending the fingerboard-mapping discipline introduced in chapter 1.
